input.problemStatement: Fragmented trial data across EDC systems, CTMS, central labs, pharmacovigilance, and CRO partners with no unified cross-study analytics, making portfolio-level signal detection, regulatory submission preparation, and AI-powered trial design impossible.
input.businessGoals: Unify all clinical trial data across the global portfolio, enable cross-study analytics and signal detection, accelerate regulatory submissions, reduce data review cycle times, and build an AI-powered trial operations platform.
input.constraints: 21 CFR Part 11 and GCP compliance required, data from CRO partners must be governed with access restrictions, CDISC SDTM/ADaM standards must be supported, and the platform must support regulatory audit trails.
input.currentState: Each trial uses a separate EDC instance (Medidata Rave, Veeva Vault), CRO partners deliver data in Excel or SAS formats on SFTP, lab data arrives as PDF reports, pharmacovigilance data lives in an Argus safety database, and CTMS is an on-premises system with no API access.
input.desiredFutureState: A unified clinical data lakehouse with CDISC-standardized data across all trials, real-time safety signal monitoring, cross-study biomarker and efficacy analytics, and AI-assisted medical review and protocol design.
input.stakeholders: Chief Medical Officer, VP Clinical Operations, VP Regulatory Affairs, Head of Biometrics, Chief Data Officer, CRO Partner Leads

architecture.title: Global Clinical Trial Data Platform Architecture
architecture.summary: A CDISC-compliant Databricks Lakehouse unifying EDC, CTMS, lab, pharmacovigilance, and CRO data across the global clinical portfolio into harmonized trial entities with cross-study analytics and AI-powered clinical insights.
architecture.solutionOverview: Trial data from EDC systems, CTMS, central labs, pharmacovigilance databases, and CRO partners flows into the lakehouse via Auto Loader for batch file ingestion and Delta Live Tables for API-based streaming. The Bronze layer preserves raw source data with full audit history required by 21 CFR Part 11. The Silver layer standardizes data to CDISC SDTM domains and harmonizes trial entities — subjects, visits, adverse events, lab results — across studies. The Gold layer materializes cross-study analytics products: safety signal tables, biomarker efficacy views, and regulatory submission packages. Unity Catalog governs access with study-level permissions and CRO partner data isolation.
assumptions: EDC systems (Medidata, Veeva) provide API access or structured data exports in CDISC-compatible formats
CRO partners deliver data via SFTP in agreed-upon formats with data sharing agreements in place
CDISC SDTM mapping specifications are available or will be developed as part of the program
Databricks will be deployed in a GxP-validated environment with appropriate SOPs
The biometrics team has SAS/R skills that can be transitioned to Python/SQL on Databricks

details.title: Trial Data Sources
details.body: Five primary source types: EDC systems (Medidata Rave, Veeva Vault EDC) as the primary trial data repository; CTMS for protocol, site, and enrollment tracking; Central Labs delivering standardized lab results in CDISC LAB format; Pharmacovigilance systems (Oracle Argus, Veeva Safety) for adverse event data; and CRO Partner data delivered as SAS datasets, Excel, or CSV via SFTP.

details.title: Ingestion Layer
details.body: Auto Loader handles batch file ingestion from SFTP drops and EDC exports with file-level audit trails. Delta Live Tables manages API-based data pulls from EDC and CTMS systems. All ingestion events are logged with timestamps and checksums for 21 CFR Part 11 compliance.

details.title: CDISC Standardization
details.body: The Silver layer applies CDISC SDTM domain mapping using configurable mapping specifications per study. Core domains include DM (Demographics), AE (Adverse Events), LB (Laboratory), VS (Vital Signs), EX (Exposure), and CM (Concomitant Medications). ADaM datasets for primary efficacy and safety analyses are derived in the Gold layer.

details.title: Cross-Study Analytics
details.body: The Gold layer enables portfolio-level analytics by harmonizing SDTM data across multiple studies using common data element mappings. Safety signal detection, biomarker efficacy analysis, and site performance benchmarking operate across the full trial portfolio.

details.title: Regulatory Submission Support
details.body: Gold layer regulatory datasets are validated against CDISC conformance rules using PINNACLE 21 Community integrated into Databricks Workflows. Submission packages including define.xml are generated automatically from Unity Catalog metadata.

nodes.label: EDC Systems
nodes.notes: Medidata Rave and Veeva Vault EDC systems storing CRF data across all active trials globally

nodes.label: CTMS
nodes.notes: Clinical Trial Management System tracking protocols, sites, investigators, enrollment milestones, and study budgets

nodes.label: Central Labs
nodes.notes: Central laboratory results in CDISC LAB format from Covance, LabCorp, and other central lab partners

nodes.label: Pharmacovigilance
nodes.notes: Adverse event and safety data from Oracle Argus and Veeva Safety, including SAEs, expedited reports, and aggregate safety data

nodes.label: CRO Partner Data
nodes.notes: Data packages from CRO partners delivered as SAS datasets, Excel, or CSV on SFTP with study-specific data sharing agreements

nodes.label: Auto Loader
nodes.notes: Incremental file ingestion from SFTP drops and EDC batch exports with schema inference and 21 CFR Part 11 audit trail

nodes.label: Delta Live Tables
nodes.notes: Declarative DLT pipelines for EDC and CTMS API integration with data quality expectations for clinical data validation

nodes.label: Bronze Layer
nodes.notes: Raw trial data in Delta format with full audit history, source checksums, and ingestion timestamps for 21 CFR Part 11 compliance

nodes.label: Silver Layer (Harmonized Trial Entities)
nodes.notes: CDISC SDTM-standardized domains (DM, AE, LB, VS, EX, CM) harmonized across all studies with study-agnostic subject and visit entities

nodes.label: Gold Layer (Cross-Study Products)
nodes.notes: Cross-study analytics products: safety signal tables, biomarker efficacy views, ADaM datasets, regulatory submission packages

nodes.label: Unity Catalog
nodes.notes: Study-level access controls, CRO data isolation with row-level security, 21 CFR Part 11 audit logs, and CDISC metadata registry

nodes.label: Databricks SQL Warehouses
nodes.notes: SQL Warehouses for clinical data review, site performance monitoring, and biostatistician self-service analytics

nodes.label: Trial Analytics
nodes.notes: Cross-study safety monitoring, enrollment tracking, site performance, and protocol deviation dashboards for clinical operations

nodes.label: AI Clinical Insights
nodes.notes: AI-powered safety signal detection, protocol optimization recommendations, and medical review acceleration using trial portfolio data

edges.label: Lab results in CDISC LAB format

refinements: Integrate PINNACLE 21 Community for automated CDISC conformance validation within Databricks Workflows
Add a natural language medical review assistant that enables clinicians to query patient narratives across all SAEs
Build a protocol deviation detection model that flags anomalous visit data patterns in real time
Implement automated define.xml generation from Unity Catalog metadata for regulatory submissions
Add external benchmarking integration using anonymized industry trial data for site performance comparison

architectAnswers.why_w1: The pharmaceutical company runs over 80 active clinical trials globally, each with a separate EDC instance, lab contract, and CRO partner. Data integration for a cross-study safety analysis requires manual extraction from 15+ systems, taking the biometrics team 6-8 weeks. Meanwhile, competitive intelligence indicates that peers with unified data platforms are running these analyses in days, potentially identifying safety signals — and making regulatory decisions — faster.
architectAnswers.why_w2: Regulatory agencies (FDA, EMA) increasingly expect sponsors to provide integrated clinical data across their portfolios to support benefit-risk assessments. The current siloed structure makes it impossible to efficiently respond to agency requests for cross-study safety analyses, creating regulatory risk and potential delays in approval timelines worth hundreds of millions of dollars.
architectAnswers.why_w3: The pipeline has 12 Phase 2 and 3 trials expected to complete over the next 3 years. Without a unified data platform, the biometrics and regulatory teams will be overwhelmed with data integration work for each submission. Standardizing on a single platform now creates the operational leverage needed to handle this volume.
architectAnswers.why_h1: CRO partners deliver data in inconsistent formats — some in SAS transport format, some in Excel, some in CSV — with no consistent variable naming or coding conventions. Each study requires a custom data transformation script, and these scripts are maintained by individual biostatisticians with no version control or reproducibility. The lakehouse replaces this with governed, versioned SDTM mapping specifications applied consistently across all studies.
architectAnswers.why_h2: Pharmacovigilance teams review adverse events in isolation from the clinical trial database, requiring manual data reconciliation between the safety database and EDC. This process is time-consuming and error-prone, creating risk of missed signals in real time. Integrating both data streams in the Silver layer enables holistic safety monitoring.
architectAnswers.why_h3: The biostatistics team spends 40-50% of their time on data management — requesting data extracts, transforming data, validating transformations — before they can do any statistical analysis. This is not a skill or capacity problem; it is a data infrastructure problem. A governed lakehouse with curated SDTM and ADaM datasets eliminates this overhead.
architectAnswers.goal_g1: The primary goal is a CDISC SDTM-compliant Silver layer covering all active trials, enabling portfolio-level safety and efficacy analytics without any per-study data transformation work. Biostatisticians should be able to query a unified subject-level dataset across 10+ trials using standard SQL within days of a new trial being onboarded.
architectAnswers.goal_g2: A critical secondary goal is automated regulatory package generation — producing SDTM and ADaM datasets with associated define.xml for each regulatory submission directly from the Gold layer, reducing manual preparation time from 6 months to 6 weeks for each NDA/BLA package.
architectAnswers.goal_o1: Operational success is measured by reducing the data-to-analysis cycle time for a cross-study safety analysis from 6-8 weeks to 3-5 days. This enables faster signal detection, more responsive pharmacovigilance, and the ability to respond to FDA/EMA queries within days rather than months.
architectAnswers.goal_o2: For clinical operations, the goal is real-time site performance monitoring — enrollment rates, protocol deviation rates, query resolution times — across all trials from a single dashboard. This enables proactive site management decisions that improve enrollment timelines and reduce trial costs.
architectAnswers.goal_a1: The platform must support at least 200 concurrent users — biostatisticians, data managers, clinical operations, and medical monitors — with query performance for cross-study analyses of 50+ million subject-visit records completing within 30 seconds using SQL Warehouses with photon acceleration.
architectAnswers.goal_a2: Safety data from pharmacovigilance must be available in the Silver layer within 4 hours of entry into the source safety database to support near-real-time safety monitoring. EDC data for routine data review should refresh with T+1 availability.
architectAnswers.goal_l1: CRO partner data must be isolated using Unity Catalog row-level security: CRO A can only see data from trials they are managing, and cannot access data from other CROs or other sponsors. Sponsor personnel access is controlled by study team membership with automated onboarding/offboarding tied to CTMS enrollment.
architectAnswers.goal_l2: 21 CFR Part 11 compliance requires a complete audit trail for all data modifications — who changed what, when, and why. Unity Catalog audit logs combined with Delta Lake transaction logs provide this trail for every transformation from Bronze to Gold. The audit log must be queryable for regulatory inspection.
architectAnswers.safe_s1: All clinical trial data containing patient identifiers must be encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+). The Databricks workspace must be deployed in a GxP-validated cloud environment with appropriate system qualification documentation (IQ/OQ/PQ) to support regulatory inspection of the computing environment.
architectAnswers.safe_s2: Subject data must be pseudonymized at ingestion — subject identifiers (patient name, date of birth, site patient number) are replaced with sponsor-assigned randomization numbers in the Bronze layer. The mapping table linking randomization numbers to patient identifiers is stored separately with restricted access, supporting both analysis and re-identification for safety follow-up.
architectAnswers.safe_a1: Study-level access controls in Unity Catalog ensure that personnel on one trial cannot access data from another trial without explicit authorization. Data access is tied to roles in the CTMS — study team members, data monitors, biostatisticians, medical monitors — each with the minimum necessary data access for their role.
architectAnswers.safe_a2: All DLT pipeline code is version-controlled in Git with code review requirements before deployment. Pipeline runs are logged with the executing user, code version, and input/output data versions for full computational reproducibility — a requirement for GCP-compliant clinical data management.
architectAnswers.safe_f1: Delta Lake ACID transactions prevent partial writes that could corrupt clinical datasets used for regulatory submission. DLT data quality expectations enforce clinical coding conventions — MedDRA AE coding, CTCAE grading ranges, CDISC controlled terminology — with automatic quarantine of non-conforming records.
architectAnswers.safe_e1: The platform has an RPO of 1 hour and RTO of 4 hours for the Bronze layer, which is the regulatory-compliant archive of all ingested source data. Delta Lake transaction logs are replicated to a secondary region. Backup restoration is tested quarterly as part of the GxP validation lifecycle.
architectAnswers.safe_e2: Pipeline failures for safety-related data (pharmacovigilance feeds, SAE reports) trigger immediate PagerDuty alerts to the safety data management team. A 30-minute SLA is defined for safety data processing failures, reflecting the regulatory importance of timely adverse event data availability.
architectAnswers.flow_f1: Data flows from clinical sites through EDC → to Auto Loader/DLT ingestion → Bronze raw preservation → Silver SDTM standardization → Gold cross-study analytics products → SQL Warehouses and AI insights. Safety data from pharmacovigilance follows a parallel high-priority path with accelerated processing to meet safety monitoring SLAs.
architectAnswers.flow_l1: SAE data from the pharmacovigilance system is the most latency-sensitive flow, processed within 4 hours of data entry. Routine EDC data — CRF completions, query resolutions, lab results — is processed nightly with T+1 availability. CTMS enrollment and site data is refreshed every 4 hours for operational monitoring dashboards.
architectAnswers.flow_o1: Data quality expectations in DLT enforce clinical coding standards at ingestion: AE terms are validated against MedDRA dictionaries, medication names are coded against WHO Drug, lab results are validated against reference ranges, and visit dates are checked against protocol-defined windows. Non-conforming records are quarantined and routed to the data management team via automated query generation.
architectAnswers.flow_w1: Workflows are orchestrated using Databricks Workflows with study-level dependency management: Bronze ingestion per study → Silver SDTM mapping per study → cross-study Gold aggregation → regulatory validation with PINNACLE 21 → dashboard refresh. Study-level isolation in orchestration prevents a data issue in one trial from blocking analytics for the broader portfolio.
architectAnswers.data_d1: Primary clinical data domains follow CDISC SDTM standards: DM (Demographics, 1 record per subject), AE (Adverse Events, time-to-event with MedDRA coding), LB (Laboratory, with LOINC and NCI codes), VS (Vital Signs, longitudinal measurement data), EX (Exposure, dosing history), and CM (Concomitant Medications, with WHO Drug coding). These domains are harmonized across all trials using a common SDTM mapping library.
architectAnswers.data_d2: Supplementary data domains include CTMS enrollment and site data, central lab results, imaging endpoint data (RECIST tumor measurements), patient-reported outcomes (PRO instruments), and pharmacokinetic sampling data. These are integrated with the core SDTM domains in the Silver layer using SUPPQUAL and custom domain patterns.
architectAnswers.data_a1: Data quality in EDC is enforced by edit checks at the point of data entry, but issues still reach the lakehouse — late corrections, missing mandatory fields, out-of-range values that passed EDC checks but fail cross-domain consistency. The Silver layer DLT pipeline applies cross-domain checks not feasible in individual EDC systems, such as verifying that adverse event start dates are within the subject's treatment period.
architectAnswers.data_a2: Terminology coding consistency is a major data quality challenge. Different studies may use different MedDRA or WHO Drug versions, and dictionary upgrades during a trial create version heterogeneity in the data. The Silver layer maintains a terminology versioning system that records which dictionary version was used for each coded term, enabling accurate cross-study comparison.
architectAnswers.data_t1: CDISC SDTM R3.4 is the canonical data standard for the Silver layer, with CDASH R2.0 as the data collection standard mapped to SDTM through configurable mapping specifications stored in Unity Catalog. ADaM ADSL (subject-level analysis dataset) and ADAE (adverse event analysis dataset) are derived in the Gold layer for primary regulatory submissions.
architectAnswers.data_t2: A common data element library maintained in Unity Catalog defines canonical variable names, controlled terminology values, and derivation rules for study-agnostic analysis. This library enables cross-study queries without per-study variable mapping by ensuring that, for example, 'Baseline ECOG Performance Status' is stored with the same variable name and coding across all oncology trials.
architectAnswers.data_a3: Clinical trial data retention must comply with ICH E6(R2) GCP guidelines (minimum 15 years post-trial completion for pivotal trials). The Bronze layer serves as the GCP-compliant archive with immutable audit history. Silver and Gold layers are retained for the active analytics lifecycle (5-7 years post-submission) with the Bronze layer providing long-term archival.
architectAnswers.data_a4: Data provenance in Unity Catalog tracks every transformation from raw EDC data in Bronze through SDTM mapping in Silver to ADaM derivation in Gold. This lineage is used during regulatory inspection to demonstrate that submission datasets are reproducible from the source data — a key GCP requirement for data integrity.
architectAnswers.fast_f1: The fastest path to value is a cross-study safety signal dashboard for the pharmacovigilance team. Adverse event data from all active trials can be ingested and SDTM-standardized in 8-10 weeks, enabling an integrated signal detection view that currently requires 6 weeks of manual data consolidation per analysis.
architectAnswers.fast_f2: Onboarding the two highest-volume EDC systems (Medidata Rave for Phase 3 trials, Veeva Vault EDC for Phase 2) in the first 12 weeks covers approximately 70% of active subject data and delivers immediate cross-study analytics value before all source systems are integrated.
architectAnswers.fast_a1: The 16-week rapid deployment plan: Weeks 1-4 focus on infrastructure, Unity Catalog study governance model, and Auto Loader configuration for the first two EDC systems. Weeks 5-8 deliver Bronze ingestion for all priority sources and Silver SDTM mapping for AE and DM domains. Weeks 9-12 deliver the safety signal dashboard and enrollment monitoring views. Weeks 13-16 add remaining SDTM domains and Gold cross-study products.
architectAnswers.fast_a2: Leverage the Databricks Life Sciences solution accelerators for CDISC SDTM mapping, which provide pre-built notebooks for the most common SDTM domains and reduce mapping development time by 50-60% compared to building from scratch. These accelerators are actively maintained and updated with new CDISC standards.
architectAnswers.fast_s1: Quick wins in the first 90 days: (1) Real-time enrollment tracker across all active studies; (2) Cross-study AE frequency dashboard for pharmacovigilance; (3) Protocol deviation trend report by site and study; (4) Query resolution time monitoring for data management. Each provides immediate operational value while the full platform is built out.
architectAnswers.fast_t1: Terraform modules for Databricks workspace provisioning, Unity Catalog configuration, and study-level access group setup can be deployed in days. A standard study onboarding template — Auto Loader job, DLT pipeline, Unity Catalog schema, and access group — enables new studies to be onboarded in 2-3 days once the template is validated.
architectAnswers.fast_t2: The Databricks CDISC SDTM mapping framework, built as a configurable Python library, allows biometrics teams to define SDTM mappings using a configuration file rather than custom code. This reduces mapping development time from 4-6 weeks per domain to 1-2 weeks and enables non-Spark-expert biostatisticians to maintain mapping specifications.
architectAnswers.run_r1: The platform is operated by a core team of 2-3 clinical data engineers, 1 platform administrator, and 1 data governance lead. Databricks Workflows handles daily pipeline orchestration. DLT pipelines are self-healing with automatic retry and data quality alerting. Study onboarding follows a standardized template with a 3-day SLA from study setup to first data availability.
architectAnswers.run_r2: Cloud infrastructure costs are managed through Databricks compute policies: DLT pipelines use Enhanced Autoscaling to right-size compute for each study's data volume; SQL Warehouses auto-suspend after 10 minutes of inactivity. Estimated annual platform cost is $800K-1.2M at 80 active studies, compared to $3-4M in current data management infrastructure and labor costs.
architectAnswers.run_u1: SDTM mapping specifications are version-controlled in Git and deployed through a CI/CD pipeline that validates the mapping against the CDISC SDTM specification before deployment. Mapping changes trigger a full reprocessing of affected Bronze data to regenerate Silver SDTM tables, with Delta Lake time travel supporting rollback if a mapping update introduces errors.
architectAnswers.run_u2: New studies are onboarded using a self-service onboarding portal that provisions the Unity Catalog schema, access groups, Auto Loader job, DLT pipeline, and initial SDTM mapping configuration from a standard template. Study Data Managers can trigger onboarding without data engineering support for standard EDC types.
architectAnswers.run_n1: The platform is deployed in a cloud environment validated to meet 21 CFR Part 11 requirements, with system qualification documentation (IQ/OQ/PQ) maintained for regulatory inspection. Network architecture uses private endpoints and no public IP addresses. All Databricks cluster traffic is routed through a firewall with approved egress rules.
architectAnswers.win_w1: Success is defined when: (1) Cross-study safety signal analysis cycle time drops from 6 weeks to 5 days; (2) Regulatory submission dataset generation time drops from 6 months to 6 weeks; (3) All active Phase 2 and 3 trials are onboarded with SDTM data available; (4) Pharmacovigilance team uses the integrated signal dashboard as their primary safety monitoring tool.
architectAnswers.win_i1: Business impact metrics: $50M reduction in regulatory submission costs over 5 years through automation; 30% reduction in trial timelines attributable to faster data availability and decision-making; Zero regulatory inspection findings related to data integrity in 3 years; $20M saved in CRO data management fees through standardized data delivery requirements.
architectAnswers.win_n1: The unified clinical data platform becomes the foundation for AI-powered drug discovery and trial design. In 3-5 years, the portfolio of historical SDTM data enables machine learning models that predict optimal patient populations, dose ranges, and endpoints for new trials — compressing Phase 2 design cycles from 18 months to 6 months and improving Phase 3 success rates through data-driven protocol optimization.

story.strategy: The pharma company chose a CDISC-first approach to the Databricks Lakehouse, recognizing that regulatory compliance is not a constraint on analytics but the foundation of it. By standardizing all trial data to SDTM in the Silver layer, the platform creates a single analytical truth that serves both regulatory submissions and cross-study research simultaneously. Unity Catalog's study-level access controls enforce CRO data isolation while enabling cross-portfolio analytics for sponsor personnel.
story.technology: The platform uses Databricks Auto Loader for EDC batch exports and SFTP-delivered CRO data, with Delta Live Tables handling real-time pharmacovigilance feeds. A configurable Python SDTM mapping library, version-controlled in Git and deployed via CI/CD, enables biostatisticians to maintain mapping specifications without Spark expertise. The regulatory submission pipeline integrates PINNACLE 21 conformance checking within Databricks Workflows to automate the validation of submission datasets.
story.outcome: Within 18 months, the platform onboarded 65 active trials across the global portfolio. Cross-study safety signal analysis time dropped from 6 weeks to 4 days, enabling the pharmacovigilance team to detect and respond to an emerging hepatic safety signal 8 weeks earlier than would have been possible with the previous process — potentially avoiding a clinical hold. The first regulatory submission package generated from the Gold layer was delivered in 5 weeks compared to the historical 6-month manual process.
story.returnValue: The platform delivered $45M in documented value in its first two years: $22M in CRO data management cost reduction, $15M in biometrics labor savings, $8M in avoided regulatory submission delays. The earlier safety signal detection enabled a proactive protocol amendment that preserved the trial rather than facing a clinical hold — a conservatively estimated $50M in trial continuation value alone. Total 3-year ROI exceeded 400%.
story.years: Over 5 years, the SDTM data corpus becomes a proprietary AI training asset. Historical trial data across 200+ completed studies enables machine learning models that predict drug-target interactions, adverse event profiles, and optimal patient stratification for new trials. The platform evolves into a drug discovery intelligence layer, with AI models guiding pipeline decisions from target identification through Phase 2 design — transforming the company's R&D productivity.

playbook.scenarioSummary: A global pharma company needs to unify fragmented trial data from EDC, CTMS, central labs, pharmacovigilance, and CRO partners into a CDISC-compliant lakehouse. The Databricks platform enables cross-study analytics, automated regulatory submissions, and AI-powered trial intelligence while maintaining 21 CFR Part 11 and GCP compliance.
businessDrivers: Cross-study safety signal detection requires 6-8 weeks of manual data consolidation, creating pharmacovigilance risk
Regulatory submissions take 6 months to prepare manually, delaying approvals worth hundreds of millions in revenue
CRO data management costs are increasing 15% annually as trial complexity grows
The pipeline of 12 upcoming Phase 3 trials will overwhelm current manual data management capacity
FDA and EMA increasingly expect integrated clinical data submissions, requiring a standardized data platform
constraints: 21 CFR Part 11 compliance requires audit trails, system validation, and access controls for all trial data
CDISC SDTM/ADaM standards are required for regulatory submissions and must be maintained across all studies
CRO partner data must be isolated with contractual data sharing restrictions enforced at the platform level
recommendedEngagementApproach: Conduct a clinical data landscape assessment to catalog all active trials, EDC systems, data formats, and CRO relationships
Map current regulatory submission process and identify the highest-effort, highest-risk bottlenecks in data preparation
Deliver a technical proof of concept ingesting one active trial's EDC data and producing SDTM DM and AE domains
Present POC results with a 5-year ROI model to the CMO, Head of Biometrics, and CDO
recommendedConversationPath: Portfolio scope: How many active trials, in what phases, with how many CRO partners?
Pain quantification: How long does a cross-study safety analysis take today? How long does submission prep take?
Compliance posture: What is the current GxP validation approach for data management systems?
Biometrics team skills: SAS vs Python/R — what is the team's comfort with modern data tools?
Executive priorities: Is faster submissions or better safety monitoring the higher-priority outcome?

discoveryQuestions.question: How many active trials are in your current portfolio across all phases, therapeutic areas, and geographies?
discoveryQuestions.whyItMatters: The scale of the portfolio determines the complexity of the ingestion layer and the value of cross-study analytics. Larger portfolios have exponentially more to gain from unified data.
discoveryQuestions.goodSignal: 40+ active trials with a growing Phase 3 portfolio and a 5-year pipeline that exceeds current capacity.
discoveryQuestions.redFlag: Fewer than 10 active trials — the ROI case for a full lakehouse platform may not justify the investment at this scale.

discoveryQuestions.question: Are your trials currently producing SDTM submissions, and do you have a centralized SDTM mapping library or does each study maintain its own mappings?
discoveryQuestions.whyItMatters: Existing SDTM experience and a mapping library dramatically accelerate Silver layer development. No SDTM experience means building standards from scratch — a major underestimate risk.
discoveryQuestions.goodSignal: A centralized biometrics team with a shared SDTM mapping library and at least one completed CDISC submission.
discoveryQuestions.redFlag: Each study creates its own SDTM mappings independently with no common library — significant rework required to standardize.

discoveryQuestions.question: What is your current approach to GxP system validation for data management systems, and who owns the validation process?
discoveryQuestions.whyItMatters: GxP validation adds 3-6 months to platform deployment and requires dedicated validation resources. Misunderstanding this requirement is a common project delay.
discoveryQuestions.goodSignal: A dedicated IT validation team with experience validating cloud-based clinical systems and a standard IQ/OQ/PQ protocol.
discoveryQuestions.redFlag: No experience validating cloud-based systems — the validation team only knows on-premises validation. Significant education and scoping required.

discoveryQuestions.question: How many CRO partners provide data, what formats do they use, and are data sharing agreements already in place governing data rights?
discoveryQuestions.whyItMatters: CRO data integration complexity scales with the number of partners and format heterogeneity. Legal data sharing agreements must precede technical integration.
discoveryQuestions.goodSignal: 3-5 preferred CRO partners with existing data sharing agreements and a standard data delivery specification.
discoveryQuestions.redFlag: 20+ CRO partners each delivering data in proprietary formats with no standard specification — integration will take 12-18 months before cross-study analytics are possible.

discoveryQuestions.question: What tools does the biometrics team currently use for analysis — SAS, R, Python — and what is their interest in modern cloud analytics tools?
discoveryQuestions.whyItMatters: Biometrics teams with SAS as their primary tool may resist Python-based Databricks workflows. Understanding current tools determines training investment and potential adoption resistance.
discoveryQuestions.goodSignal: Team uses R and Python for exploratory analysis alongside SAS for submission deliverables — strong foundation for Databricks adoption.
discoveryQuestions.redFlag: Pure SAS shop with no Python or cloud experience and strong cultural attachment to SAS — change management is as important as technical implementation.

problemFraming.statement: A global pharma company cannot efficiently detect safety signals, prepare regulatory submissions, or optimize its trial portfolio because clinical trial data is fragmented across dozens of EDC systems, CRO partners, and safety databases with no unified, standards-compliant data layer for cross-study analytics.
desiredOutcomes: Cross-study safety analysis cycle time reduced from 6-8 weeks to 3-5 days
CDISC SDTM-compliant data available for all active trials within 6 months of platform launch
Regulatory submission package generation automated to produce submission-ready datasets in 6 weeks instead of 6 months
Real-time safety monitoring dashboard across the full portfolio for the pharmacovigilance team
AI-powered trial optimization capabilities built on the unified data corpus
assumptions: EDC systems provide API or structured batch export access
CRO partners can be contracted to deliver data in standardized formats
The biometrics team will adopt Python/SQL workflows for SDTM mapping with training support
Cloud deployment is acceptable to the GxP validation and compliance teams
nonNegotiables: 21 CFR Part 11 compliant audit trails for all data transformations
CDISC SDTM as the canonical standard for the Silver layer
Study-level and CRO-level data isolation enforced at the platform level
unknowns: Depth of GxP validation requirement for the cloud platform and timeline impact
CRO partners' willingness to change data delivery formats
Biometrics team capacity to maintain SDTM mapping specifications during platform transition
problemFraming.framingStatement: By building a CDISC-first Databricks Lakehouse, we transform a fragmented global trial portfolio into a unified clinical intelligence platform that detects safety signals in days instead of weeks, generates regulatory submissions automatically, and creates an AI-ready data corpus that will compound in value with every trial completed.

architectureOptions.name: CDISC-First Databricks Medallion Lakehouse
architectureOptions.summary: A full medallion architecture with CDISC SDTM standardization in Silver and ADaM derivation in Gold, governed by Unity Catalog with study-level access controls and 21 CFR Part 11 compliant audit trails.
whenToUse: Global pharma with 20+ active trials across multiple phases and CRO partners
Cross-study safety analytics required by pharmacovigilance and regulatory affairs
CDISC submission requirements for FDA and EMA regulatory submissions
AI use cases requiring a large, harmonized clinical dataset for model training
benefits: SDTM standardization in Silver enables true cross-study analytics without per-analysis data transformation
Unity Catalog study-level access controls enforce CRO data isolation contractually and technically
Delta Live Tables with DLT expectations automates CDISC conformance checking at ingestion
Regulatory submission automation reduces a 6-month manual process to weeks
tradeoffs: SDTM mapping development requires significant upfront biometrics investment per study
GxP validation of the cloud platform adds 3-6 months to deployment timeline
Biometrics teams with pure SAS background require significant training and change management
platformMapping: Auto Loader → EDC batch export and CRO SFTP file ingestion
Delta Live Tables → pharmacovigilance streaming and CTMS API integration
Silver Layer → CDISC SDTM domain standardization
Gold Layer → ADaM dataset derivation and regulatory package generation
Unity Catalog → study-level RBAC, CRO isolation, 21 CFR Part 11 audit logs

items: SFTP gateway with file integrity checks
CDISC SDTM domain tables (DM, AE, LB, VS, EX, CM)
Medical dictionary coding tables (MedDRA, WHO Drug)

talkTrack: Think of our CDISC Silver layer as the Rosetta Stone for your trial portfolio — once every trial's data speaks the same SDTM language, cross-study analytics require zero additional data transformation work.
Unity Catalog's study-level access controls mean your CRO contracts are enforced in the data platform, not just in legal agreements — CRO A literally cannot see CRO B's data.

Regulatory submissions generated from the Gold layer are reproducible from source data — every derivation is traceable through Unity Catalog lineage, giving your regulatory team confidence in the data integrity.

customerQuestions.question: How does Databricks handle 21 CFR Part 11 compliance for GxP data?
customerQuestions.bestAnswer: Databricks provides the technical controls required for 21 CFR Part 11: audit trails via Unity Catalog and Delta Lake transaction logs, access controls with electronic signatures for data modification workflows, and system validation support documentation. You are responsible for the procedural controls (SOPs, training records) and the IQ/OQ/PQ validation, but Databricks provides the technical foundation and qualification documentation to support your validation effort.
customerQuestions.notes: Be honest that validation responsibility sits with the customer, but emphasize the Databricks documentation and support available. Reference other pharma customers who have successfully validated.

customerQuestions.question: Our biometrics team only knows SAS. How do we transition?
customerQuestions.bestAnswer: Databricks SQL Warehouses accept standard SQL, so your biostatisticians can start querying SDTM data immediately without learning Python or Spark. For SDTM mapping development, we provide a Python library with a configuration-file interface that biometrics staff can use without becoming Python developers. We recommend a 6-month parallel-run period where new platform outputs are validated against existing SAS workflows before cutting over.
customerQuestions.notes: Meet the team where they are. SQL first, Python optionally. Validate in parallel to build trust. Change management is as important as technology.

risks.title: GxP Validation Timeline Underestimation
risks.description: GxP validation of a cloud-based clinical data platform is often underestimated. Regulatory affairs and quality teams may impose requirements that extend validation timelines by 6-12 months.
risks.mitigation: Engage the QA and regulatory affairs teams in the scoping phase to define validation requirements. Use Databricks's pre-built qualification documentation to accelerate IQ/OQ. Build validation into the project plan from day one with a dedicated validation workstream.

risks.title: CRO Data Format Standardization
risks.description: CRO partners may be unwilling or unable to change their data delivery formats to meet the lakehouse ingestion requirements, requiring custom transformation logic for each partner.
risks.mitigation: Include data delivery format standardization requirements in new CRO contracts. For existing CROs, develop generic adapters in Auto Loader that handle common format variations without requiring CRO-side changes. Prioritize the 3-5 CROs responsible for 80% of active trial data.

risks.title: SDTM Mapping Quality and Maintenance
risks.description: SDTM mapping specifications are complex and change with protocol amendments, dictionary upgrades, and new CDISC standards. Inadequate mapping maintenance leads to submission datasets that fail conformance checks.
risks.mitigation: Establish a biometrics-owned SDTM mapping library with version control and peer review requirements. Integrate PINNACLE 21 conformance checking into the CI/CD pipeline for mapping changes. Assign a mapping library owner role with dedicated maintenance responsibility.

deliverables.name: Clinical Data Platform Architecture Blueprint
deliverables.purpose: A comprehensive technical design for the CDISC-compliant Databricks Lakehouse including ingestion patterns, SDTM mapping framework, Unity Catalog governance model, and regulatory submission pipeline.
outline: Ingestion architecture (Auto Loader, DLT patterns per source type)
Unity Catalog governance model (study-level RBAC, CRO isolation)
Gold layer regulatory submission pipeline design
GxP validation strategy and documentation framework
Deployment roadmap by study onboarding priority

deliverables.name: SDTM Mapping Library Specification
deliverables.purpose: A specification for the configurable SDTM mapping library that enables biometrics teams to define and maintain study-specific mappings without custom code.
outline: Mapping configuration schema by SDTM domain
Controlled terminology integration (MedDRA, WHO Drug, LOINC, NCI)
Version control and change management procedures
Study onboarding template and SLA definition

executiveSummary.sponsorReady: We are building a unified clinical data platform on Databricks that will connect your entire global trial portfolio into a single CDISC-compliant data foundation. This platform will reduce cross-study safety analysis time from 6 weeks to 4 days, automate regulatory submission preparation, and create an AI-ready data corpus that will power the next generation of trial design and portfolio decision-making.
executiveSummary.thirtySecond: Your trial data is fragmented across dozens of systems, forcing your biometrics team to spend months manually integrating data for every safety analysis and regulatory submission. The Databricks Lakehouse puts all trial data in one CDISC-compliant platform, automating the integration work and freeing your scientists to do science.
executiveSummary.twoMinute: Today your pharmacovigilance team needs 6-8 weeks to pull together a cross-study safety analysis because the data lives in 15+ different systems in incompatible formats. Your biometrics team spends 40% of their time on data management rather than analysis. Your regulatory submissions take 6 months to prepare manually, and every hour of delay is revenue not earned. The Databricks Clinical Trial Lakehouse solves all of this. We ingest data from all your EDC systems, CRO partners, central labs, and safety databases into a Bronze layer with 21 CFR Part 11 compliant audit trails. Delta Live Tables transforms this data to CDISC SDTM standards in Silver — the same standards you submit to the FDA. Your Gold layer produces analysis-ready ADaM datasets and automatically validates them with PINNACLE 21. The result: a cross-study safety analysis that takes 4 days instead of 6 weeks, a regulatory submission that takes 6 weeks instead of 6 months, and an AI-ready data corpus that will power your next decade of R&D.
successIn12Months: 65+ active trials onboarded with SDTM data available in the platform
Cross-study safety signal analysis cycle time under 5 days
First regulatory submission package generated automatically from the Gold layer
Pharmacovigilance team using integrated safety dashboard as primary monitoring tool
$20M in documented cost savings from biometrics and CRO data management efficiency

meetingChecklist: Confirm Head of Biometrics, VP Regulatory Affairs, and CDO are all present
Prepare a timeline showing current vs future state for safety analysis and submission preparation
Bring examples of CDISC SDTM mapping framework and how it handles real-world EDC data
Have a demo showing cross-study AE analysis on sample SDTM data
Prepare the 21 CFR Part 11 compliance mapping and GxP validation approach
Know which CRO partners are most strategic and their current data delivery formats
nextSteps: Schedule a clinical data landscape assessment workshop with the biometrics and data management teams
Request sample data extracts from the 2 highest-priority active trials for POC development
Identify a clinical trial to use as the POC study with a willing data manager as champion
Deliver a 6-week POC proposal producing SDTM DM and AE domains from the POC study
Schedule a regulatory affairs review of the 21 CFR Part 11 compliance documentation
whiteboardTalkTrack: Draw the current state: 10+ EDC systems, CRO SFTP drops, safety database, CTMS — all disconnected
Show the pain: a safety analysis request arriving, then draw the 6-week manual integration process
Introduce the lakehouse: a single ingestion layer collecting from all sources into Bronze
Walk through SDTM transformation in Silver — explain why CDISC as the canonical standard enables cross-study queries
Show Gold layer: ADaM datasets, safety signal tables, regulatory packages — all automated
Highlight Unity Catalog: study-level locks on the data, CRO isolation, audit logs for inspectors
Close with the AI opportunity: this SDTM corpus becomes a training dataset for trial optimization models
workshopPlan: Morning Session 1 (90 min): Trial data landscape inventory — catalog all active trials, source systems, CRO relationships, and current integration points
Morning Session 2 (60 min): Pain point quantification — calculate current manual effort for safety analysis and submission preparation in FTE-hours
Afternoon Session 1 (90 min): Architecture walkthrough — CDISC SDTM framework, ingestion patterns, Unity Catalog governance model
Afternoon Session 2 (60 min): GxP validation strategy — define validation scope, responsibilities, and timeline
Wrap-up (30 min): POC study selection, success criteria, and next steps

objections.objection: We've always used SAS for regulatory submissions. FDA won't accept Python-derived datasets.
objections.response: FDA accepts submission datasets in any compliant format — what matters is CDISC SDTM/ADaM conformance, not the tool used to produce them. PINNACLE 21 conformance checking is tool-agnostic. Many pharma companies now produce FDA-accepted submissions from Python and R pipelines. We include PINNACLE 21 validation in the Gold layer pipeline to ensure every dataset meets FDA conformance requirements before submission.

objections.objection: Our CROs won't change how they deliver data. This platform assumes standardized inputs we can't control.
objections.response: We design the ingestion layer to handle format heterogeneity — Auto Loader adapters can ingest SAS transport files, Excel, CSV, and JSON without requiring CRO-side changes. The transformation to SDTM happens inside the lakehouse, not at the source. Over time, we recommend including standardized delivery requirements in new CRO contracts, but the platform works with current delivery formats immediately.

objections.objection: Validating a cloud platform for GxP use will take 2 years and cost more than the platform itself.
objections.response: That concern reflects older experiences with on-premises system validation. Databricks provides pre-built qualification documentation (System Description, Functional Risk Assessment) that dramatically reduces validation effort. Our pharma implementation partners have validated Databricks workspaces for 21 CFR Part 11 use in 4-6 months. We can share reference validation documentation from comparable implementations.
